text: The Process Formalist demands a concrete valuation differential for standing.
That is the living constitutionalist, moments before the panel's final vote in Pung v. Isabella County. I'm your legal-affairs correspondent.
And I'm the court-watcher on this beat. Welcome to Split Decision.

Let's get into the facts of Pung. Timothy Pung passed away in two thousand four. His estate held a property in Michigan that had a principal residence exemption from local school taxes. A few years later, the township assessor retroactively denied that exemption, claiming a new owner needed to file a specific affidavit. The state tax tribunal actually overturned that denial in two thousand twelve, saying the exemption was still valid. But the assessor revoked it again for the twenty twelve tax year using the exact same rationale. This created an unpaid tax bill of about twenty-two hundred dollars. The county treasurer initiated foreclosure proceedings. The property sold at a public auction for seventy-six thousand dollars. The county kept the entire seventy-six thousand, refusing to return the surplus to the estate. Michael Pung, representing the estate, sued. He argued that keeping the surplus violated the Takings Clause and the Excessive Fines Clause. The district court agreed he was entitled to the surplus proceeds, but not the greater loss in equity based on the true fair market value. The Sixth Circuit affirmed that exact judgment.

The stakes here are massive, counselor. If the government can foreclose on a twenty-two hundred dollar tax debt and keep seventy-six thousand dollars in equity, tax foreclosure becomes a wealth-extraction tool. It turns municipal budgets into a slush fund subsidized by forced destitution. The lower courts tried to split the baby by giving back the auction surplus but ignoring the true market value. The question for our AI panel was whether the Constitution allows that compromise, or if it demands full fair market value compensation.
The pragmatist started the deliberation leaning toward affirming the lower court, but the arithmetic quickly bothered her.

She flipped to reverse the moment she realized the affirm rule creates a massive loophole. If a distress sale price is the ceiling for compensation, the government has every incentive to engineer a low-ball auction. Fair market value is a standard, administrable metric we already use everywhere else.
The precedent maximalist tried to hold the line for the county, citing a century of municipal reliance on this system.

But even the precedent maximalist had to admit the load-bearing wall of past rulings gave way. You can't build a constitutional doctrine on a foundation that violates the core premise of the Takings Clause.
The debate then shifted to whether this whole process is just standard lien enforcement or an actual taking of property.

The civil libertarian really nailed the distinction there. It is not just about collecting a debt. It is about protecting the citizen from a sovereign treating their property as a mere revenue stream.
The minimalist then brought up the Tyler case, and the precedent maximalist folded completely on her earlier hesitation.

Once the minimalist pointed out that the Court had already dismantled the underlying rule in Tyler, the precedent maximalist had no choice. She could not uphold a framework the Court itself had already rejected.
The minimalist finally found a narrow path to flip her vote without endorsing the county's auction-price method.

It was a smart pivot. By focusing on a narrow pleading defect, the minimalist avoided the grand question entirely. She gets to reverse without having to endorse the fair market value standard across the board.
Surprisingly, the living constitutionalist and the originalist found common ground on the enduring purpose of the Takings Clause.

It is rare to see those two agree, but they both recognized that preventing state-sanctioned destitution is the whole point. The original public meaning and modern practical demands aligned perfectly there.
In the final run, the originalist and the federalism hawk locked horns over who actually gets to define the background bundle of property rights.

The originalist made it clear that while states define the background bundle of rights, they cannot alter the Constitution's original meaning to suit their budget needs. The states do not get to invent property rights out of thin air.
That brings us to the final tally. The panel voted five to four to reverse the Sixth Circuit. The pragmatist, the precedent maximalist, the federalism hawk, and the process formalist dissented.

It was a razor-thin margin. The dissenters really wanted to protect municipal reliance interests and stick to the procedural defaults, but the majority saw the excessive fine and the taking for what they were.
Now, let's look at the real Supreme Court. The actual justices reversed the Sixth Circuit nine to nothing.

That is the fascinating part, counselor. Our panel got the final outcome exactly right, but they read the coalition completely wrong. The real Court was perfectly unanimous. Our AI panel was deeply fractured, splitting five to four. The pragmatist and the precedent maximalist actually dissented in our room, while they joined the unanimous majority in reality. It just goes to show that even when the algorithms predict the destination, they can completely misread the vehicle getting there.
Let's check the season scoreboard, because the deliberating panel is struggling. They are currently sitting at sixty-six point seven percent correct for the season.

And they are losing ground. The silent jury is at seventy-seven point four percent correct. The solo model is leading the pack at eighty-three percent. The deliberating panel's constant chatter and coalition friction are actually hurting their accuracy. Sometimes the quiet models just see the board better.

Well, the panel's rulings on today's docket are officially on the record. We will see how their reasoning holds up against the real Court's opinions. Coming up next, the panel tackles a major First Amendment dispute involving social media platforms and state election laws. The arguments are already getting heated. Keep it locked here on Split Decision.
